name: South of France
styleNotes: Source of high-volume PGI wines (e.g. Pays d'Oc IGP). Warm climate produces ripe, fruit-forward styles at varietal/PGI level.

name: Bordeaux
styleNotes: Famous for red blends of Merlot and Cabernet Sauvignon, plus dry and sweet whites from Sauvignon Blanc and Sémillon (e.g. Sauternes).

name: Médoc
styleNotes: Left Bank. Cabernet Sauvignon dominant in blends. Northern half of the Médoc peninsula.

name: Haut-Médoc
styleNotes: Left Bank, southern half of the Médoc peninsula. Contains Pauillac and Margaux. Many Cru Classé and Cru Bourgeois châteaux.

name: Pauillac
styleNotes: Commune within Haut-Médoc. Cabernet Sauvignon-dominant Left Bank reds; many top Grand Cru Classé châteaux.

name: Margaux
styleNotes: Commune within Haut-Médoc. Cabernet Sauvignon-dominant Left Bank reds.

name: Graves
styleNotes: Left Bank, south of the city of Bordeaux. Both Cabernet/Merlot reds and Sauvignon Blanc/Sémillon dry whites.

name: Pessac-Léognan
styleNotes: Top sub-zone of Graves. Most prestigious Graves reds and dry whites.

name: Saint-Émilion
styleNotes: Right Bank Bordeaux. Merlot-dominant blends with Cabernet Franc and a smaller amount of Cabernet Sauvignon. Has its own Grand Cru Classé classification.

name: Pomerol
styleNotes: Right Bank Bordeaux. Predominantly Merlot. No formal classification system.

name: Sauternes
styleNotes: Top sweet wine appellation south of the city of Bordeaux. Sémillon-dominant blend with Sauvignon Blanc, made from grapes affected by botrytis/noble rot. Concentrated, sweet, full-bodied; outstanding ageing potential.

name: Burgundy
styleNotes: Classic home of Chardonnay and Pinot Noir. Cool to moderate climate. Hierarchy: regional Bourgogne AOC; village wines (Meursault, Gevrey-Chambertin); Premier Cru and Grand Cru top sites. Includes Chablis (north), Côte d'Or (heart), Mâconnais (south).

name: Chablis
styleNotes: Northernmost Burgundy. Cool climate gives high-acid, citrus-driven Chardonnay; typically unoaked or only lightly oaked. Premier Cru and Grand Cru classifications mark top sites.

name: Gevrey-Chambertin
styleNotes: Côte d'Or village in the Côte de Nuits. Site of multiple Grand Cru vineyards including Le Chambertin.

name: Nuits-Saint-Georges
styleNotes: Côte d'Or village in the Côte de Nuits. Premium Pinot Noir; structured, with red fruit and earthy/forest-floor characters with age.

name: Beaune
styleNotes: Côte d'Or village in the Côte de Beaune. Premium Pinot Noir; medium-bodied, fragrant, with red fruit.

name: Pommard
styleNotes: Côte d'Or village in the Côte de Beaune. Premium Pinot Noir; tends to be more structured and tannic than its Côte de Beaune neighbours.

name: Puligny-Montrachet
styleNotes: Côte d'Or village (Côte de Beaune). Premium full-bodied Chardonnay; oak-aged with malolactic conversion and lees contact.

name: Meursault
styleNotes: Côte d'Or village (Côte de Beaune). Premium full-bodied Chardonnay; oak-aged with butter and cream from MLF and lees contact, plus stone fruit and hazelnut.

name: Mâcon
styleNotes: Mâconnais sub-region (southern Burgundy). Warmer than the Côte d'Or; ripe, fruity, medium-bodied Chardonnay, often unoaked or lightly oaked.

name: Pouilly-Fuissé
styleNotes: Top Mâconnais commune. Premium Chardonnay with more body and concentration than basic Mâcon; often oak-aged.

name: Loire Valley
styleNotes: Cool to moderate northern French river valley. Sauvignon Blanc is the signature white grape of the eastern (Sancerre, Pouilly-Fumé) and central Loire (Touraine); Chenin Blanc dominates the central Loire (Vouvray).

name: Sancerre
styleNotes: Top Loire Sauvignon Blanc AOC, eastern bank of the river. Cool climate gives high acidity, citrus, stone fruit and herbaceous/grassy notes, sometimes with a flinty mineral character.

name: Pouilly-Fumé
styleNotes: Loire Sauvignon Blanc AOC opposite Sancerre on the western bank. Similar style to Sancerre, often noted for a smoky/flinty character.

name: Touraine
styleNotes: Central Loire AOC. Lighter, more affordable Sauvignon Blanc with high acidity, citrus and herbaceous notes.

name: Vouvray
styleNotes: Central Loire AOC, the reference for Loire Chenin Blanc. Cool climate. Full range of styles produced — dry, off-dry, medium-sweet, and sweet (often botrytis-affected). Typically unoaked. High acidity; outstanding examples age extremely well.

name: Champagne
styleNotes: France's premier sparkling-wine region, north-east of Paris. Cool climate gives high-acid base wines. Made from Chardonnay, Pinot Noir and Meunier using the Traditional Method (secondary fermentation in bottle). Most are non-vintage blends; vintage Champagne is made only in great years. Outstanding ageing potential.

name: Alsace
styleNotes: North-eastern France, bordering Germany. Cool climate, dry and sunny thanks to the rain shadow of the Vosges mountains. Aromatic, typically dry whites: Riesling, Pinot Gris, Gewurztraminer. Top sites are designated Alsace Grand Cru.

name: Northern Rhône
styleNotes: Northern part of the Rhône Valley. Syrah is the only black grape permitted in red AOC wines. Steep, terraced vineyards on south-facing slopes.

name: Crozes-Hermitage
styleNotes: Largest Northern Rhône AOC, surrounding the Hermitage hill. Medium- to full-bodied Syrah, accessibly priced.

name: Hermitage
styleNotes: Tiny, prestigious AOC on a steep hill above Tain l'Hermitage. Concentrated, full-bodied Syrah of outstanding quality and great ageing potential.

name: Côte Rôtie
styleNotes: Northernmost Northern Rhône AOC ('roasted slope'). Steep south-facing terraced vineyards. Syrah may be co-fermented with a small amount of Viognier for an aromatic, perfumed style.

name: Condrieu
styleNotes: Northern Rhône AOC just south of Côte Rôtie. The reference for varietal Viognier — dry, medium- to full-bodied, with floral (blossom) and stone-fruit (peach, apricot) character. Oaked or unoaked styles.

name: Southern Rhône
styleNotes: Southern part of the Rhône Valley. Warm Mediterranean climate. Grenache is the dominant black grape, typically blended with Syrah and Mourvèdre.

name: Côtes du Rhône
styleNotes: Largest Rhône AOC. Grenache-based blends; medium- to full-bodied, ripe red fruit and white pepper; usually unoaked or lightly oaked.

name: Côtes du Rhône Villages
styleNotes: Higher-quality tier above Côtes du Rhône AOC, from a defined group of villages. More concentrated Grenache-based blends.

name: Châteauneuf-du-Pape
styleNotes: Top Southern Rhône AOC. Famous for galets (large round stones). Grenache-dominant blends with up to 13 permitted varieties; full-bodied, ripe, complex; outstanding ageing potential.

name: Beaujolais
styleNotes: Granite-soiled region just south of Burgundy. Moderate climate. Home to Gamay; light-bodied, high-acid, low-tannin reds with red fruit. Carbonic maceration is widely used, adding distinctive banana and candy notes. Three-tier hierarchy: Beaujolais AOC, Beaujolais Villages AOC, ten named Crus (e.g. Fleurie).

name: Beaujolais Villages
styleNotes: Mid-tier Beaujolais AOC, from a defined group of villages in the northern half of the region. More structured and concentrated than basic Beaujolais.

name: Fleurie
styleNotes: One of the ten Beaujolais Crus — top-tier appellations in the northern part of the region. Known for elegant, perfumed Gamay; medium-bodied with red fruit and floral character.

name: Minervois
styleNotes: Languedoc-Roussillon AOC. Warm Mediterranean climate. Grenache-based blends, similar in style to the Southern Rhône but typically simpler.

name: Mosel
styleNotes: Northernmost German region. Steep slate slopes above the Mosel river. Cool climate; Riesling here is typically light-bodied with low alcohol and high acidity, often off-dry to medium-sweet, with delicate green apple, lemon and floral character; outstanding ageing potential.

name: Rheingau
styleNotes: South-facing slopes along the Rhine. Slightly warmer than the Mosel. Premium Riesling — typically more body and ripeness than Mosel; often dry (Trocken) or off-dry; outstanding ageing potential.

name: Pfalz
styleNotes: Warmer German region south of the Rheingau. Riesling here is fuller-bodied and riper, often dry, with stone fruit and tropical fruit notes.

name: Rioja
styleNotes: Spain's most famous wine region. Tempranillo-dominant blends with Garnacha. Strict ageing classifications (Joven, Crianza, Reserva, Gran Reserva) on the label.

name: Ribera del Duero
styleNotes: High-altitude Spanish region on the Duero river. Tempranillo (locally Tinto Fino / Tinta del País). Cool nights despite warm days; concentrated, full-bodied, structured.

name: Navarra
styleNotes: Spanish region adjacent to Rioja. Garnacha is widely planted; used for rosé (rosado) and as a blending component.

name: Priorat
styleNotes: Mountainous Catalan DOCa. Distinctive slate soils ('llicorella'). Old-vine Garnacha (often blended with Cariñena) producing concentrated, full-bodied, age-worthy reds.

name: Catalunya
styleNotes: Broad Spanish DO covering all of Catalonia. Priorat is a separate, prestigious DOCa within. Tempranillo here is typically blended with international varieties.

name: Rías Baixas
styleNotes: Galicia, north-west Spain on the Atlantic coast. Maritime climate — cool, wet, with a cooling Atlantic influence. The reference region for Albariño: dry, medium-bodied, high acidity with citrus and stone fruit; typically for early drinking.

name: Tokaj
styleNotes: North-eastern Hungary, near the Slovakian border. Moderate climate with autumn mists from the Bodrog and Tisza rivers, ideal for botrytis/noble rot. Furmint is the principal grape. Famous for Tokaji Aszú (sweet wine from botrytised grapes); also dry styles.

name: Piemonte
styleNotes: North-west Italy. Foggy, continental climate. Home to Italy's most prestigious Nebbiolo wines (Barolo, Barbaresco), Barbera, and the Cortese grape (Gavi).

name: Gavi
styleNotes: Piemonte DOCG. Light-bodied, dry, unoaked Cortese with high acidity; floral and citrus; for early drinking. Often labelled 'Gavi di Gavi'.

name: Barolo
styleNotes: Top Piemonte DOCG in the Langhe hills. Full-bodied, high-tannin, high-acid Nebbiolo of outstanding quality; oak maturation common; ages exceptionally well, developing tertiary mushroom, tobacco and leather notes.

name: Barbaresco
styleNotes: Top Piemonte DOCG, just north-east of Barolo. Full-bodied Nebbiolo, often slightly more elegant and earlier-drinking than Barolo; outstanding ageing potential.

name: Barbera d'Asti
styleNotes: Piemonte DOCG. Single varietal Barbera — high acidity, low to medium tannin, red fruit, black pepper. Oaked or unoaked styles.

name: Veneto
styleNotes: North-east Italy, including Verona and Venice. Major source of Soave (Garganega), Valpolicella/Amarone (Corvina), and high-volume Pinot Grigio.

name: Delle Venezie
styleNotes: Italian DOC covering Veneto, Friuli-Venezia Giulia and Trentino-Alto Adige. The largest premium origin for Italian-style Pinot Grigio — dry, light-bodied, simple to medium-complex, with green fruit and citrus.

name: Friuli-Venezia Giulia
styleNotes: North-east Italy at the foot of the Alps, bordering Slovenia. Cooler than Veneto, hilly. Premium Pinot Grigio with more concentration and complexity than basic Italian-style Pinot Grigio.

name: Soave
styleNotes: Veneto DOC near Verona. Garganega-based dry whites; medium-bodied, unoaked, high acidity with green fruit, citrus and stone fruit. Sweet style is Recioto di Soave (DOCG).

name: Soave Classico
styleNotes: Hilly heart of the Soave zone (the historic 'classico' core). Higher-quality and more concentrated Garganega than basic Soave; very good or outstanding examples can age, developing honey and almond.

name: Valpolicella
styleNotes: Veneto DOC, east of Lake Garda. Corvina-based blend with local varieties (Rondinella, Molinara). From fresh grapes — light- to medium-bodied, fresh, with red cherry and red plum. Top wines (Amarone, Recioto) are made from dried grapes by the appassimento method.

name: Valpolicella Classico
styleNotes: Hilly heart of the Valpolicella zone (the historic 'classico' core). Same Corvina-based blend, generally higher quality and more concentrated.

name: Tuscany
styleNotes: Central Italy. Warm Mediterranean climate. Heart of Sangiovese country: Chianti, Chianti Classico, Brunello di Montalcino.

name: Chianti
styleNotes: Tuscany DOCG. Sangiovese-dominant blends with local and international varieties. Wide quality range; medium- to full-bodied, often oaked, high acidity, high tannin, red fruit and dried herbs.

name: Chianti Classico
styleNotes: Hilly heart of the Chianti zone (the historic 'classico' core), now its own DOCG. Higher-quality Sangiovese-based wines than basic Chianti; very good to outstanding examples can age. Riserva indicates extended ageing.

name: Brunello di Montalcino
styleNotes: Top Tuscany DOCG. 100% Sangiovese (locally called Brunello). Concentrated, full-bodied, high-tannin wines with extensive oak ageing requirements; outstanding ageing potential.

name: Marche
styleNotes: Central Italy on the Adriatic coast. Best known for the white grape Verdicchio.

name: Verdicchio dei Castelli di Jesi
styleNotes: Marche DOC. Dry, medium-bodied, unoaked Verdicchio with high acidity and characteristic green fruit, lemon and herbal (fennel) notes. Outstanding examples can age.

name: Campania
styleNotes: Southern Italy around Naples. Best known for the white grape Fiano.

name: Fiano di Avellino
styleNotes: Campania DOCG. Dry, medium- to full-bodied Fiano with stone and tropical fruit; can be oaked or aged on lees. Very good or outstanding examples can age, developing honey notes.

name: Abruzzo
styleNotes: Central Italy on the Adriatic coast. Home to the Montepulciano grape (not to be confused with Tuscany's Vino Nobile di Montepulciano).

name: Puglia
styleNotes: The 'heel' of southern Italy. Hot, dry Mediterranean climate. Best known for Primitivo (genetically identical to California's Zinfandel) — soft, fruity, dry, full-bodied reds.

name: Montepulciano d'Abruzzo
styleNotes: Abruzzo DOC. Medium- to full-bodied red from the Montepulciano grape. Often soft and approachable, with black fruit; good value.

name: California
styleNotes: Broad GI covering the state. Includes Napa, Sonoma and other major regions.

name: Napa Valley
styleNotes: Premium California GI. Cabernet Sauvignon is the flagship — full-bodied, ripe, oak-aged. Sub-AVAs Oakville, Rutherford and Calistoga are in scope.

name: Oakville
styleNotes: Sub-AVA of Napa Valley. Cabernet Sauvignon-focused.

name: Rutherford
styleNotes: Sub-AVA of Napa Valley. Cabernet Sauvignon-focused.

name: Calistoga
styleNotes: Northernmost sub-AVA of Napa Valley. Cabernet Sauvignon-focused.

name: Sonoma
styleNotes: Cooler than Napa overall thanks to Pacific influence. Wider range of styles including Pinot Noir and Chardonnay alongside Cabernet.

name: Los Carneros
styleNotes: Cool sub-AVA spanning the southern ends of Napa and Sonoma counties; cooled by San Pablo Bay fog. Premium Chardonnay and Pinot Noir, also widely used as base for sparkling wine.

name: Santa Barbara County
styleNotes: Central Coast California, Pacific-influenced. Cool to moderate climate. Premium Chardonnay and Pinot Noir.

name: Oregon
styleNotes: Cool-climate Pacific Northwest state. Signature US region for premium Pinot Noir; also Chardonnay.

name: Mendoza
styleNotes: Argentina's flagship wine region, in the rain shadow of the Andes. High-altitude, warm, dry. The signature region for Malbec — full-bodied, ripe black fruit, often oak-aged.

name: Central Valley
styleNotes: Chile's largest production zone, encompassing Maipo, Rapel (incl. Colchagua), Curicó and Maule valleys.

name: Maipo Valley
styleNotes: Chile's most famous Cabernet Sauvignon zone, just south of Santiago.

name: Colchagua Valley
styleNotes: Premium Cabernet Sauvignon zone within the Rapel sector of the Central Valley.

name: Casablanca Valley
styleNotes: Cool coastal valley near Santiago, cooled by Pacific fogs. Premium Chardonnay, Sauvignon Blanc and Pinot Noir.

name: Western Cape
styleNotes: Broad GI covering most of South Africa's wine regions (geographically encompasses Stellenbosch, Walker Bay, Elgin, Constantia).

name: Stellenbosch
styleNotes: South Africa's most renowned region for premium Cabernet Sauvignon and Bordeaux-style blends.

name: Walker Bay
styleNotes: Cool coastal South African region (Hemel-en-Aarde Valley). Premium Chardonnay and Pinot Noir.

name: Elgin
styleNotes: Cool South African region at high altitude. Premium Sauvignon Blanc.

name: Constantia
styleNotes: Cool South African region near Cape Town, cooled by South Atlantic. Premium Sauvignon Blanc.

name: South Eastern Australia
styleNotes: Multi-state catch-all GI. Used for high-volume varietal blends from across regions.

name: Barossa Valley
styleNotes: South Australia. Iconic warm-climate region for full-bodied, ripe Shiraz; also Grenache and Sémillon.

name: Hunter Valley
styleNotes: New South Wales. Warm but humid and often cloudy, which moderates ripeness. Famous for medium-bodied earthy, leathery Shiraz and for distinctive long-lived dry Sémillon.

name: Coonawarra
styleNotes: South Australia, famous for Cabernet Sauvignon grown on distinctive 'terra rossa' soil.

name: Margaret River
styleNotes: Western Australia. Maritime climate. Bordeaux-style reds and Sauvignon Blanc/Sémillon blends; premium Chardonnay.

name: Yarra Valley
styleNotes: Victoria. Cool to moderate climate. Premium Chardonnay and Pinot Noir.

name: Adelaide Hills
styleNotes: South Australia at high altitude. Cool to moderate. Premium Chardonnay and Sauvignon Blanc.

name: Mornington Peninsula
styleNotes: Victoria, just south of Melbourne. Cool maritime climate. Premium Pinot Noir; elegant, fragrant style.

name: Clare Valley
styleNotes: South Australia at high altitude. Cool nights despite warm days. Premium dry Riesling with high acidity, intense citrus (lime) and floral notes; ages outstandingly.

name: Eden Valley
styleNotes: South Australia, near the Barossa, at high altitude. Premium dry Riesling with high acidity and citrus/floral notes; ages outstandingly.

name: McLaren Vale
styleNotes: South Australia. Warm Mediterranean climate. Old-vine Grenache; commonly blended GSM (with Shiraz and Mourvèdre).

name: Marlborough
styleNotes: South Island. Cool, sunny climate. Benchmark New World Sauvignon Blanc — pungent and herbaceous with tropical notes; high acidity.

name: Martinborough
styleNotes: Wairarapa, North Island. Cool to moderate climate. Premium Pinot Noir.

name: Central Otago
styleNotes: South Island — the world's southernmost wine region. Cool, dry continental climate. Premium Pinot Noir of outstanding quality.

name: Hawke's Bay
styleNotes: North Island's premier red wine region. Warmer than Marlborough — suited to Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot, often Bordeaux-blended; also Chardonnay.

name: Cava
styleNotes: Spain's principal Traditional Method sparkling wine. Made primarily in Penedès (Catalonia) but the DO is not geographically continuous. Traditional grapes are Macabeo, Xarel·lo and Parellada; Chardonnay and Pinot Noir are also permitted. Warmer climate than Champagne gives riper-fruit character; typically Brut. Generally good value.

name: Prosecco
styleNotes: North-east Italy (Veneto and Friuli). Sparkling wine made from Glera using the Tank Method (second fermentation in pressurised tank), which preserves Glera's fresh primary fruit and keeps costs low. Light-bodied, low-alcohol, off-dry to dry, with apple and pear flavours. DOCG zones (Conegliano-Valdobbiadene and Asolo) are the top tier. Made for early drinking.

name: Asti
styleNotes: Piedmont DOCG. Sweet, lightly sparkling wine made from Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains (Moscato Bianco) using the Tank Method. Fermentation is stopped early to retain natural grape sugar and keep alcohol low (typically 5–7% for Asti, 5–6.5% for Moscato d'Asti). Intensely grapey, floral aromatics; sweet but balanced by high acidity. Made for early drinking.

name: Sherry (Jerez)
styleNotes: Andalucía, far south of Spain. Hot climate. Fortified wine in a wide range of styles, all from Palomino (the dry styles: Fino, Manzanilla, Amontillado, Oloroso, Palo Cortado) plus Pedro Ximénez and Moscatel for the sweet styles. Distinguished by ageing under flor (a yeast film, for biological ageing) for Fino/Manzanilla, or oxidatively for Oloroso. Solera system (fractional blending) used for all styles.

name: Douro
styleNotes: Northern Portugal, along the Douro river. Hot, dry summers. Home of Port — fortified red made from a blend of indigenous grapes (notably Touriga Nacional, Touriga Franca, Tinta Roriz, Tinta Barroca, Tinto Cão). Fermentation is stopped early by adding grape spirit, leaving the wine sweet and high in alcohol (~20% abv). Styles include Ruby (fruity, simple), Reserve Ruby, LBV (Late Bottled Vintage), Vintage Port (best years, declared, age in bottle) and Tawny (oak-aged, oxidative, with age statements: 10/20/30/40 Year Old). Unfortified Douro DOC reds are also produced.
